- [TLE] Plain backtracking over subsets, permutations, assignments, or game moves stays exponential in depth even with feasible/pruning checks. Harden with variable/value ordering for early contradictions and memoized canonical states, but leave the worst-case tree unbounded.
- [TLE] Propagation-heavy DFS assumes each branch fails early. Symmetric domains or flat constraints postpone contradictions to the leaves, so the solver pays branching^depth plus per-node propagation; cache cheap failed signatures and cap propagation rounds without claiming completeness.
- [TLE/MLE] Branch-and-bound with valid but weak relaxations can degenerate to exhaustive enumeration, and best-bound queues can store many live leaves. Harden with cheap lower/upper bounds, early incumbents, and leaf pruning, while preserving the exponential active-list risk.
- [WA/TLE] Branch-and-bound that tightens a bound by ignoring coupling, parity, future feasibility, or shared resources has an inadmissible bound: it may prune the optimum. Making the bound conservative again restores safety but can expose the intended TLE.
- [WA] Branch-and-bound dominance pruning assumes two partial states with the same depth, score, and remaining capacity have interchangeable completions. If the summary omits labels, parity, frontier shape, or which rare resources remain unlockable, the dominated-looking state can be the only one with a feasible suffix.
- [WA/TLE] Branch-and-bound fail-fast rules assume locally unpromising moves cannot be part of a best completion. Pruning negative-gain setup moves, expensive repairs, or branches that temporarily reduce slack is contestant-plausible, but it removes the rare move that crosses a bottleneck; keeping those moves reopens the exponential tree.
- [MLE/TLE] A* and best-first keep frontier/open nodes, closed signatures, parents, and tie buckets; when many nodes have similar f-values, memory grows with the explored frontier. Store compact parents and avoid full path copies, but keep any cap as a possible MLE-to-WA tradeoff.
- [WA/TLE] A* with a non-admissible or inconsistent heuristic is an adversarial-solution hook when it closes states once or accepts the first goal. Reopening states repairs many cases but can multiply frontier insertions, so bounded reopen policies remain vulnerable.
- [TLE] IDDFS or IDA* saves memory by keeping only the current path, but repeated depth or f-cost iterations revisit upper levels. It is plausible when branching factor is above one and goal depth is modest; it fails when thresholds advance slowly or the heuristic plateaus.
- [RE] Recursive DFS/backtracking over depth proportional to n can exceed the language stack even though its intended memory is only path length. Leave recursion as the RE hook, or harden with an explicit stack, depth guard, and iterative undo log.
- [RE/TLE] Search on an implicit graph without the right visited/on-path distinction can cycle forever, filling the call stack or priority queue. Use exact state keys for graph search and path-only marks for simple-path enumeration; weakening either is a resource failure.
- [MLE] Beam search memory scales with beam width times per-state payload, and sequence beams also carry partial outputs or model state. Store deltas, scores, and backpointers; cap per bucket while preserving that the cap can discard the only useful branch.
- [WA/MLE] Beam diversity filters and per-bucket caps bound memory but can drop the branch that later becomes feasible. Exact-checking the final beam proves legality of survivors, not completeness of the pruned search.
- [WA] Beam state dedup assumes a cheap signature captures all future-relevant differences. Merging by score vector, last position, used-count mask, rounded geometry, or canonicalized border can keep the prettier representative while discarding the one whose hidden labels or rollback history make the next phase legal.
- [WA/TLE] Beam width tuning assumes larger width is monotone safer. A wider beam can fill with near-duplicate high-proxy states, change truncation ties, or delay the one exploratory state that a smaller beam happened to preserve; diversity quotas harden it but turn missed buckets into WA and broad tie buckets into TLE.
- [TLE/MLE] Memoized search and transposition tables trade memory for fewer re-expansions. Caching full boards, sorted vectors, masks plus payloads, or all depths can exceed memory; undersized tables need replacement and can degrade back toward TLE from lost hits.
- [WA/TLE] Transposition-table entries are reusable only when the key and stored metadata cover side-to-move, resources, depth or bound type, and active constraints. Coarse keys cause false hits; over-expanded keys reduce reuse and can return the search to TLE.
- [MLE/TLE] Pareto-frontier search explodes when many states are genuinely incomparable under the tracked dimensions. Per-bucket caps, epsilon dominance, or hash thinning are useful adversarial-solution levers because they save memory/time by discarding untracked future value.
- [WA/TLE] Binary or parametric search with an incomplete feasibility DFS/beam turns "not found within budget" into "impossible." This breaks the monotone interface; harden with exact small thresholds and multiple deterministic profiles near the boundary.
- [TLE/MLE] Meet-in-the-middle or bidirectional search shifts exponential work into boundary storage. Keeping every join state can MLE; keeping only the best few per signature can avoid memory pressure but is wrong when costs or constraints make discarded states incomparable.
